data.title: CHAPTER 3 — PROMPTING AS SPECIFICATION
blocks: You send Claude a message: "Can you improve this introduction?"
Chapter 3 — Prompting as Specification — Two minutes later you have a rewritten paragraph. It is polished. The sentences are shorter. The word choice is cleaner.

S02.meta: // SLIDE 02 — WHAT THIS CHAPTER LETS YOU DO

data.title: WHAT THIS CHAPTER LETS YOU DO.
blocks: · Identify the six components of a well-specified prompt
· Convert a vague request into an inspectable work order
· Use structure (plain headings or XML tags) to separate task, context, constraints, source material, output format, and e
· Diagnose a failing prompt before rewriting it
· Know when to ask Claude to elicit missing requirements before producing an answer
data.section: WHAT THIS CHAPTER LETS YOU DO

S03.meta: // SLIDE 03 — THE CORE CONCEPT: A PROMPT IS

data.title: THE CORE CONCEPT: A PROMPT IS A WORK ORDER
blocks: What to do
What to use
What to avoid
What the finished work should look like
data.section: THE CORE CONCEPT: A PROMPT IS

S04.meta: // SLIDE 04 — THE SIX COMPONENTS OF A WELL-S

data.title: THE SIX COMPONENTS OF A WELL-SPECIFIED PROMPT.
blocks: Task — What should Claude do?
Context — What does Claude need to know?
Source material — What evidence should Claude use?
Constraints — What must Claude avoid?
data.section: THE SIX COMPONENTS OF A WELL-S

S06.meta: // SLIDE 06 — WHY XML? WHY NOT JUST WRITE IT

data.title: WHY XML? WHY NOT JUST WRITE IT OUT?.
blocks: You do not have to use XML tags. For a simple prompt with one or two components, plain prose or Markdown headings work f
Why XML? Why Not Just Write It Out? — XML tags become useful when your prompt has multiple sections and the model might confuse them (Anthropic, "Use XML tags
data.section: WHY XML? WHY NOT JUST WRITE IT

data.title: THE REQUIREMENT-GATHERING CONVERSATION.
blocks: Not every task starts with a complete specification. Sometimes you do not know exactly what you need until you start. In
The Requirement-Gathering Conversation — "I need help evaluating a research methods section, but I am not sure what angle is most useful. Ask me three questions

S08.meta: // SLIDE 08 — THE HUMAN GATE: WHAT TO VERIFY

data.title: THE HUMAN GATE: WHAT TO VERIFY
blocks: The purpose why this work matters and what it will be used for
The source constraints what evidence is valid, what is not
The evaluation criteria what "good" means in your institutional, scientific, or professional context
The final decision whether the output is acceptable for use
· Knowledge of your organization's unstated norms
data.section: THE HUMAN GATE: WHAT TO VERIFY

data.title: COMMON MISTAKES
blocks: Asking for a finished artifact before asking for a diagnosis.
Using role prompts as a substitute for a task specification.
Treating XML or Markdown tags as magic.
Asking Claude to "make it better" without defining better.
Accepting a fluent output as a correct one.

data.title: THE CORE CONCEPT: A PROMPT IS A WORK ORD
blocks: The research community has spent years studying prompting as a family of techniques for adapting language models to task
A good specification tells the contractor — human or AI — four things:
